rules.title: Supabase project URL detected
rules.description: Your Supabase project URL was found in frontend assets. This is normal in itself (the frontend must include it); we use it to run further checks on your database's front door.

remediation.summary: No action needed — this is what we use to locate your project.
steps: No action required

rules.title: service_role key leaked to the frontend
rules.description: A Supabase service_role key was found in browser-visible code/requests. This key bypasses all RLS and is equivalent to database superadmin privileges — anyone can read and write all of your data. This is the most severe class of incident (the same root cause as CVE-2025-48757).

remediation.summary: Rotate the service_role key immediately and make sure it never appears in the frontend.
steps: Sign in to the Supabase console and go to Project Settings → API
Click Reset/Roll next to the service_role key to rotate it
Remove the key from your frontend code and use it only server-side (Edge Function/backend)
Check your Git history; if it was ever committed, purge it and rotate as well
remediation.consolePath: Project Settings → API → Project API keys

rules.title: Supabase Secret Key (sb_secret_) leaked
rules.description: A new-style Supabase secret key (sb_secret_ prefix) was found in frontend assets. This key has server-level privileges and must never be exposed anywhere the browser can see it.

remediation.summary: Rotate the secret key in the console and remove it from the frontend.
steps: Supabase console → Project Settings → API Keys
Roll the leaked secret key
Use the new key only in server-side environment variables

rules.title: anon public key exposed (confirm RLS is enabled)
rules.description: A Supabase anon public key was found. The anon key is designed to be publicly visible, so the risk is not the exposure itself but this: if the corresponding tables don't have RLS enabled, anyone with this key can read and write your data directly. Be sure to confirm that RLS is enabled on all tables that hold user data.

remediation.summary: Confirm RLS is enabled on all business tables and connect Supabase for a deep audit.
steps: Supabase console → Authentication → Policies: confirm RLS is enabled table by table
Write least-privilege policies for tables holding user data, avoiding USING (true)
Connect a read-only credential in InsightElk to run a deep RLS audit

rules.title: Supabase Publishable Key (sb_publishable_)
rules.description: A new-style publishable key (sb_publishable_ prefix) was found. It is designed to be public and is equivalent to the anon key; the risk depends on whether RLS is configured correctly.

remediation.summary: Just confirm that RLS is configured correctly.
steps: Confirm that business tables have RLS enabled with least-privilege policies configured

rules.title: Stripe live Secret Key leaked
rules.description: A Stripe live secret key (sk_live_) was found. An attacker could use it to issue real charges and refunds and to read customer and payment data.

remediation.summary: Roll the key immediately in the Stripe Dashboard.
steps: Sign in to the Stripe Dashboard → Developers → API keys
Roll the leaked secret key
Secret keys must live only server-side; the frontend may use only the publishable key

rules.title: OpenAI API Key leaked
rules.description: A likely OpenAI API key was found. A leak can be abused to run up large bills. AI apps often hardcode the key into frontend model calls, which is a common pitfall.

remediation.summary: Revoke the key and proxy model calls through your server instead.
steps: OpenAI platform → API keys → Revoke the leaked key
Create a new key that stays server-side; have the frontend call through your own backend proxy
Set usage limits and alerts

rules.title: Anthropic API Key leaked
rules.description: A likely Anthropic (Claude) API key (sk-ant- prefix) was found; a leak can be abused to run up charges.

remediation.summary: Revoke it in the Anthropic console and switch to a server-side proxy.
steps: console.anthropic.com → API Keys → revoke the leaked key
Store and call it server-side instead

rules.title: AWS Access Key leaked
rules.description: An AWS Access Key ID (AKIA prefix) was found. If the matching secret is also leaked, an attacker could operate your cloud resources.

remediation.summary: Deactivate/delete the access key in IAM and investigate its use.
steps: AWS console → IAM → Users → Security credentials
Deactivate/Delete the leaked Access Key
Replace it with temporary credentials or a server-side secrets-management solution

rules.title: Google API Key leaked
rules.description: A Google API key (AIza prefix) was found. An unrestricted key may be abused to incur charges.

remediation.summary: Set HTTP referrer / API restrictions on the key, or rotate it.
steps: Google Cloud Console → APIs & Services → Credentials
Add application and API restrictions to the key, or rotate it outright

rules.title: GitHub Token leaked
rules.description: A GitHub token (ghp_/gho_/ghu_/ghs_/ghr_ prefix) was found and may be used to access your repositories.

steps: GitHub → Settings → Developer settings → Tokens → Revoke

rules.title: Private key file contents leaked
rules.description: A PEM private key block (-----BEGIN ... PRIVATE KEY-----) was found. A leaked private key can enable signature forgery and service impersonation.

remediation.summary: Immediately rotate the corresponding key pair and remove the private key from the code.
steps: Generate a new key pair and replace all references
Thoroughly remove the private key from the frontend and from repository history

rules.title: Likely hardcoded password
rules.description: A hardcoded credential in the form password: "..." was found. It may be a database or third-party service password.

remediation.summary: Move the credential into server-side environment variables and rotate it.
steps: Remove the hardcoded credential
Use environment variables instead and rotate the leaked credential

rules.title: Firebase config exposure
rules.description: A Firebase database address was found. If the Firebase security rules are configured for public read/write, this will lead to a data leak.

remediation.summary: Review your Firebase security rules and disallow anonymous read/write.
steps: Firebase console → Realtime Database/Firestore → Rules: tighten permissions

rules.title: Likely high-entropy secret (catch-all detection)
rules.description: A high-entropy string was found that may be a secret/token not precisely matched by a rule. This is a catch-all notice; please confirm manually.

remediation.summary: Manually confirm whether it's a secret; if so, rotate it and move it out of the frontend.
steps: Confirm what the string is used for
If it's a secret, rotate it and move it server-side

rules.title: Source map exposure
rules.description: A sourceMappingURL was found. Exposing source maps in production leaks your original source code, making it easier for attackers to find vulnerabilities and hardcoded secrets.

remediation.summary: Disable production source maps in your build configuration.
steps: Vite: build.sourcemap = false
After redeploying, confirm the .map files are no longer publicly accessible

rules.title: Supabase Auth config is publicly readable
rules.description: Supabase's /auth/v1/settings endpoint can be read anonymously; we use it to check your signup and email-confirmation configuration.

remediation.summary: No action needed; this only serves as the basis for subsequent Auth config checks.
steps: No action required

rules.title: Anyone can self-service sign up
rules.description: Your Supabase Auth has not disabled self-service signup (disable_signup=false). If this isn't what your product intends (e.g. an internal tool or invite-only), it may be abused for mass signups.

remediation.summary: If this isn't a public product, disable self-service signup or switch to invite-only.
steps: Supabase console → Authentication → Providers/Settings
Turn off "Allow new users to sign up" (if applicable)

rules.title: Email confirmation disabled (auto-confirm)
rules.description: mailer_autoconfirm=true was detected, meaning users can activate accounts without verifying their email. Attackers can register using other people's email addresses and impersonate identities, and it's also more prone to mass abuse.

steps: Supabase console → Authentication → Providers → Email
Enable Confirm email
